item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote at "heraldry containing various corporate logos". I'd love to see that. On a field, the words PET, gules, and SMART, azure…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dude when you make an article about an american transnational corporation, you are supposed to add a twist, not just describe the already existing company.
+1 tho

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. I was getting really into the negotiations and such until I read

All but the Duke Yankee Candle and Count GrubHub, leave us.

…and then couldn't take the rest of it too seriously. Absolute masterpiece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i loved reading this. it's got a really good edge of complex and clean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is inspired. I want to know more about them. What does history look like from their point of view? Do they own any anomalies?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… madness is one of the most insanely entertaining things I've ever read. My only regret is I will never experience this again for the first time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I somehow missed footnote 3 until I got to the bottom, so my facial expression shot from bemused to horrified grimace.

I also love how you covered my train of thought with the new. Like, surely the biggest goal (due to it being the biggest threat to containment) is stopping them killing people, but we're just not at that point in the story yet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
High Lord of Global Credit and Duke of PetSmart

aaaaaand that's when I upvoted.

This is absolutely delightful and I'll have a hard time looking at paper guillotines the same now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is such a joy of an article all around. Footnote 3 in particular might be one of my favorite sentences on the entire website. Hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was intentionally put in for that jarring reason (to bring the reader back to the fact that this is a modern American guy, if a strange one) but it was something that sounded much smarter to my 2am-addled brain, and I didn't continue it consistently, so I'll cut it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to try and defend my object class choice as I have a more lax/flexible definition of the classes than a lot of others, but I realised that even by my standards this leans more towards Keter than Euclid, so I've changed it.

The lack of response from authorities or organised response from others is intended to be covered by the line about secondary anomalous properties which suppress the response of others, as an extension of the compulsion cliche. If you think this is an unsatisfying cop-out that's entirely fair, but I did see that plot hole and try to paper it over.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Feudalism to kleptocracy is no long walk so there are amusing themes here, but the implementation is problematic. This is a completely uncontained Keter that kills people — at the very least its classification as Euclid is wrong. I get that the point is that it’s amusing for the cliche contest, but it’s a gaping wound in reality that the Foundation would either contain or be forced to neutralize, and that’s a bigger deal than the central gag.

Keep in mind, not everyone is in on the gag. If the feudalists try to employ violence against someone not infected by the cognitohazard and the victim fights back successfully, you’ll have a lot of dead bodies and complete containment breach, followed quickly by more dead bodies. And how have authorities not arrested all concerned yet?

For me, the diplomacy log is far less important than those plot holes, so when I got that instead of an explanation, I became distinctly unhappy with the writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great, easy +1.

WEST: (sighs) Well, one cannot expect those without ancient lineage to… okay, you must address His Majesty correctly, of course, speak only when prompted, avoid mention of your quarterly figures, pay respect to your own liege without insinuating any supremacy. You do bring tribute, I hope?

The "okay" in this line feels very out of place with the rest of the character's dialogue. It's both too modern and too casual and breaks the tone of the rest of the line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I try to skirt as close to real things as possible

This is my favorite aspect of your style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another classic MMG skip. Good work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I try to skirt as close to real things as possible, so it's good to know this reminded you of weird CEO habits.

Glad you enjoyed the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks. I usually wait a bit before changing the title (to save it from being changed back if the article crashes and burns) but this looks healthy enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I might come back to this at some point in the future - I initially planned to write out some of the treaty in faux medieval speak but ran out of time and energy before the contest deadline. Glad you enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't write many comedy SCPs, as you end up getting jokes about Dodd-Frank and the like, so good to know that was appreciated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I cannot describe how much I love this. +1

edit: Fixed!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, just yes!
That transcript was amazing, I'd love to read more of it.
I'd upvote more if I could, but take my +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its a fantastic build-up from describing a normative company to 2 paragraphs down the line describing the exact anomaly in question. Normally, I feel perturbed when writers withhold the exactness or the hints at something anomalous requiring a monolith like the Foundation to cover it up, but that does not apply when the execution is so good.

Yours was well executed. Especially when playing on the theme of old-timey aristocracy melding with modern-day hierarchies and business culture. Reminds me of those fringe stories and rituals you hear every so often about CEOs eating only sardines to conserve their omega 3 intake or eating McDonalds meals before or after a transaction.

+10 if I could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy cow this is ambitious, I wouldn't mind seeing this become a new GoI.
The description actually had me thinking it was too ambitious, so I was prepared for the worst.

Then came the dialogue.

+1 to infinity

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very creative and engaging, interesting mix of modern and medieval. Interesting take on the compulsion effect. +1

The poisonous words of Dodd and Frank have even caused rumours of schism.

Amazing.

item.title: 'High Finance'
item.content:encoded:
This is an entry for the Cliché Contest (compulsion effect that makes you do bad things/kills you).

Image source
